narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and a middle-class family background, you've put real effort into building a strong profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 59%, tipping the scales toward rejection to protect the university's bar passage rates. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a concerning dip in later academic performance compared to peers. This outweighed your stronger undergraduate GPA, which was above the average of 3.21 and actively pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your LSAT score of 26, significantly lower than the dataset average of about 37, also contributed notably toward the failure risk, standing out as weaker than what successful bar takers usually show. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3rd—below average but not drastically so—helped a bit by nudging toward passing, as did your middle income bracket and full-time status, which align with the majority of students who succeed. In essence, while your early college record and background offered some reassurance, the model zeroed in on your lower later high school deciles and LSAT as red flags compared to typical admitted students who pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven lens on bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your high school record through additional coursework or explanations, and highlighting any upward trends in applications to other schools. You're clearly capable, given your GPA edge, so targeted improvements could shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you've got this.
